name: Wendover
short_description: BLM public land near the Nevada border offering agate and chalcedony hunting in a remote high desert setting. Free rockhounding with no permit required.
description: Wendover sits on Utah BLM land in the remote high desert region near the Nevada border, characterized by sparse vegetation and wide open basin terrain. This area is part of the Great Basin physiographic province, where ancient lake beds and volcanic activity have left behind excellent opportunities for agate and chalcedony hunters. The site is popular with rockhounders seeking quality specimens without the cost of fee dig operations, making it accessible for collectors of all budget levels. Expect to find translucent to opaque agates, banded chalcedony, and occasional jasper specimens weathered out of ancient deposits. The terrain is relatively open with good visibility, allowing you to scan large areas for material scattered on the surface and in dry washes.
history: The Wendover area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-aged volcanic and sedimentary rocks host agate and chalcedony deposits. These silica-rich minerals formed in ancient hydrothermal systems and host rocks that have been exposed and weathered by millions of years of erosion, releasing specimens that now weather out onto the surface.
tips: Search along dry wash bottoms and around small hillside outcrops where erosion naturally concentrates lighter-colored agate and chalcedony specimens. Bring a 10x loupe to examine color banding and translucency in the field - the best pieces often show defined bands or windows of light transmission. Focus on areas with visible white or tan chalky material in the soil, as this indicates silica-rich zones. Early morning light helps spot specimens that might otherwise blend into the tan desert surface.
terrain_notes: High desert basin terrain at approximately 5,100 feet elevation with sparse low shrub vegetation typical of the Great Basin. Terrain is mostly flat to gently rolling with scattered small rock outcrops. Walking is generally easy on hardpan and sandy soil, though loose rock and small stones are common. No permanent water sources. Parking areas are undeveloped - roadside pullouts near promising collecting zones.
best_season: April - May and September - October. These shoulder seasons offer mild temperatures and low precipitation typical of the Great Basin high desert. Summer heat can exceed 90 degrees F, and winter brings occasional snow and freezing temperatures that make collecting uncomfortable.
written_directions: From Elko, Nevada, take US 93 south toward the Utah border. Continue approximately 40 miles south on US 93 into Utah, passing through Wendover. BLM collecting areas are accessible from various pullouts along and near US 93 south of the Wendover townsite. Look for dirt road access points and natural pullouts on the west side of the highway. High-clearance vehicle recommended for accessing some secondary roads, though main access is via US 93.
road_conditions: US 93 is paved and well-maintained. Secondary access roads to collecting areas are dirt and may have washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle helpful but not required for main collecting zones.
parking_notes: Roadside pullouts and informal parking areas along US 93. No developed parking facility. Space is usually available, but areas can attract multiple rockhounders on weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collection must be from surface only - do not dig into hillsides or excavate outcrops.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Surface collection only
nearest_services: Elko, Nevada - approximately 40 miles north. Full services including gas, restaurants, and supplies. Wendover town itself has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Parking is at roadside pullouts with easy walking to collecting areas. No wheelchair-accessible facilities. Terrain is stable hardpan and sandy soil with minimal obstacles. Good visibility makes navigation simple. Suitable for families and those with limited mobility, though heat exposure is a consideration.

faq.question: What gems can you find rockhounding near Wendover, Utah?
faq.answer: Wendover is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz. The site is particularly good for banded agate and translucent chalcedony specimens that weather out of ancient hydrothermal deposits. Most pieces are found as surface collections in washes and along hillsides.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Wendover, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Wendover. This is BLM public land where personal collecting is allowed free of charge under FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 lbs per day and must collect from the surface only.

faq.question: How far is Wendover rockhounding site from Elko, Nevada?
faq.answer: Wendover is approximately 40 miles south of Elko via US 93. The drive takes about 45 minutes from Elko, making it accessible as a day trip from northern Nevada.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Wendover?
faq.answer: April - May and September - October offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Summer temperatures exceed 90 degrees F in this high desert location, while winter can bring freezing temperatures and snow. Spring and fall provide mild weather ideal for full-day collecting trips.

faq.question: What tools do you need for rockhounding agate at Wendover?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x loupe to examine banding and translucency in the field, comfortable hiking boots for the rocky terrain, a collection bag to stay within the 25 lb daily limit, and a GPS device or map since cell service is spotty. Sunscreen and plenty of water are essential in this exposed desert environment.

faq.question: Is Wendover good for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Wendover is excellent for beginners. The open terrain and easy walking make it accessible for all skill levels. Agates and chalcedony are relatively common surface finds, and there is no cost to collect. Bring a loupe to help identify quality pieces and learn to spot color banding.

meta_title: Wendover Rockhounding - Find Agate in Utah
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Wendover, Utah for agate, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit required. 25 lbs per day allowed.
